description:
secretx-keyring/src/lib.rs:286-335 `integration_roundtrip`:

The cleanup at lines 325-327 (`if let Ok(entry) = keyring::Entry::new(svc, acct) { let _ = entry.delete_credential(); }`) only runs if execution reaches it. If any assertion between lines 313 and 322 fails (e.g. line 318 `assert_eq!(got.as_bytes(), b"test-secret-value")`) or line 313 `panic!("put failed: ...")` fires, the test panics and the kernel keyring retains an entry named `secretx-test/roundtrip` for the lifetime of the persistent keyring expiration window (default ~3 days).

Subsequent test runs hit lines 298-301 which DO clean up before the next put, so this self-heals on the next run — but it leaves dirty state in the kernel for any process inspecting the test user's keyring in the meantime, and on a developer's machine that does not re-run tests, the entry persists.

This is more of a hygiene concern than a hazard, but it is a maintainer-burden pattern: a future contributor adding a second integration test that also uses `secretx-test` as service name will collide with leftover entries from a previously panicked run.

Resolution options: (a) wrap the body in a struct with `Drop` that performs cleanup unconditionally; (b) use a unique service name per run (e.g. include a UUID or PID); (c) do the cleanup in a `scopeguard`-style closure. Option (a) is most idiomatic Rust.
title: Integration test cleanup not panic-safe — leaves entry on kernel keyring on assertion failure
